title: Lebanon's President Makes Historic Washington Visit, Russia Pummels Ukraine With Ballistic Barrage, Israeli Ministers March on Gaza for Settlements
subhead: Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun made the first Washington visit by a Lebanese leader in 17 years to seek Trump's help ending the Israel-Hezbollah conflict, Russia launched one of the largest ballistic missile attacks of the war on Ukrainian cities, and Israeli cabinet ministers led a march demanding Jewish settlements be rebuilt inside Gaza.

sections.heading: Third US Soldier Killed as Iran-US War Enters Day Eight
sections.body:
A US service member was killed and another injured during a controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian attack drone in northern Iraq on Saturday, according to US Central Command. The death follows Friday's attack on a base in Jordan that killed two American soldiers and left another missing, with Centcom reporting that 'unidentified remains' had been found at that location and were undergoing verification. The US death toll in the conflict has now reached 17.

The two sides exchanged strikes for an eighth consecutive day. US strikes hit an under-construction nuclear power plant in Darkhoveyn in south-western Iran as well as Qeshm Island, Iranian media reported. The UN's nuclear watchdog called for restraint but noted the Darkhoveyn site was 'in the very early stages' and contained no nuclear material during its last inspection. Iran retaliated by launching missiles toward Jordan, three of which were shot down by Jordan's military while a fourth fell in a remote area. Jordan summoned Iran's charges d'affaires to protest what its foreign ministry called 'unjustified and blatant Iranian attacks'.

In Kuwait, an electricity and water plant struck on Saturday was hit again, causing a fire. The Gulf Cooperation Council accused Tehran of targeting civilian infrastructure. Iran's IRGC also said two vessels that ignored warnings not to transit the Strait of Hormuz were involved in 'an accident', warning all vessels on 'unsafe routes' would 'certainly face accidents'. Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei issued a statement calling for 'sacred unity' and accusing the US of having a signature that is 'utterly worthless and devoid of credibility'.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict is widening beyond Iran and the US to directly involve Jordan, Kuwait, and commercial shipping in Hormuz. Each additional US death raises domestic pressure on Trump, while Iran's willingness to strike Gulf states and Jordan signals it will not confine retaliation to American targets.

sections.heading: Russia Launches One of Its Largest Ballistic Missile Attacks on Ukraine
sections.body:
Russia carried out a wave of ballistic missile and drone strikes on Ukrainian cities overnight, killing at least eight people and injuring dozens, according to Ukrainian officials. Four people were killed in Kharkiv, an elderly woman died in Kyiv, two more in Zaporizhzhia, and one in Sumy. President Zelensky described the raid on Kyiv as 'one of the most massive ballistic attacks' since the full-scale invasion began in 2022. The Ukrainian air force said the assault involved Iskander and hypersonic Zircon missiles alongside 125 drones, with air defences shooting down 18 of 41 missiles and intercepting 108 drones.

Separately, Ukraine's navy said five people were killed when a Turkish-owned civilian cargo ship carrying grain was hit by Russian missiles in the Black Sea. Russia has not commented on that strike. Ukraine also said it hit three Russian oil tankers in the Black Sea and three oil depots in the Stavropol region, with Zelensky describing them as targets that 'finance Russian aggression'. The Caspian Pipeline Consortium confirmed its terminal at Novorossiysk was hit, suspending oil loading operations.

The exchange underscores a grim symmetry: Russia is escalating its bombardment of Ukrainian cities while Ukraine continues to degrade Russia's fuel export infrastructure. Zelensky repeated his call for more ballistic missile interceptors from European allies, calling air defence 'our constant and top priority right now'.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia's use of hypersonic Zircon missiles alongside Iskanders represents a significant escalation in strike complexity. Meanwhile, Ukraine's Black Sea strikes on oil tankers and the Caspian Pipeline terminal directly threaten Russia's remaining export revenue streams.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Conscription Crisis Turns Violent
sections.body:
Ukraine's military ombudswoman says attacks on recruitment officers have become 'regular' and 'a major problem' as the country struggles to mobilise men in the fifth year of Russia's full-scale invasion. The National Police Service has recorded more than 600 assaults on recruitment officers to date, with aggression becoming 'increasingly threatening'. In Lviv this month, around 200 protesters surrounded officers attempting to detain a suspected draft dodger and smashed and overturned their car.

The backlash cuts both ways. Ukraine's Human Rights Commissioner recorded more than 6,000 complaints of abuse and unlawful detention by recruiters last year, double the 2024 tally. University professor Kyrylo Ogdansky, who is exempt from the draft, was beaten by recruiters in Dnipro last November and spent two weeks in hospital. A serving soldier, Vasyl Krupych, was attacked with a crowbar while searching for draft dodgers and spent six weeks in hospital.

Zelensky's recent sacking of Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov — who had begun introducing reforms including fixed-term contracts and better salaries for recruits — means attempts to address the crisis will stall. Ukraine now has only an acting defence minister and no clear solution to a mobilisation problem that is eroding both military manpower and public trust in the war effort.
sections.whyItMatters: More than 600 assaults on recruiters and 6,000 complaints of abuse signal that Ukraine's conscription system is breaking down from both ends — citizens resisting violently and officials coercing brutally. With the defence minister sacked and no replacement, the institutional capacity to fix this is missing at a moment when manpower shortfalls directly affect frontline outcomes.

sections.heading: Israeli Ministers March on Gaza Demanding Return of Settlements
sections.body:
Israeli cabinet members including Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich and National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir joined a march towards the Gaza border on Sunday to demand the rebuilding of Jewish settlements removed in 2005. The 'March of Thousands', organised by the settler group Nachala, called for the reconstruction of Elei Sinai, Dugit, and Nisanit — three settlements evacuated during Israel's disengagement from Gaza. Smotrich and Ben-Gvir, both of whom have been sanctioned by several European countries, urged Israelis not to miss a 'historic opportunity' to rebuild settlements. Ben-Gvir posted a video with the caption 'Gaza is ours'.

Prime Minister Netanyahu's Likud party was listed among the rally's sponsors, despite Netanyahu's insistence that Gaza will not be resettled. The march is the latest sign that settlement advocacy has moved from the political margins into Israel's mainstream discourse, with Defence Minister Israel Katz recently telling Channel 14 he intends to establish three Nahal outposts in northern Gaza. An Al Jazeera analysis notes that Israel has expanded the 'Yellow Line' — the demarcation of Israeli-controlled areas within Gaza — to encompass 70 percent of the Strip. A University of Pennsylvania poll found 82 percent of Jewish Israelis support the expulsion of Palestinians from Gaza.

The settlement push comes nine months into a US-brokered ceasefire during which Israeli attacks have killed at least 1,127 Palestinians, according to Al Jazeera, including 265 children. The UN reports that only 20 percent of needed aid is entering Gaza. With Israeli elections set for October 27, analysts expect further escalation as ministers compete for right-wing votes.
sections.whyItMatters: Serving cabinet ministers openly calling for settlements in Gaza marks a qualitative shift in Israel's posture — from denying resettlement plans to championing them on the border. The timing ahead of October elections, combined with the 70% territorial expansion of the Yellow Line, suggests settlement pressure is not just rhetoric but a policy trajectory being built on the ground.

sections.heading: Lebanon's President Meets Rubio Ahead of Trump Talks
sections.body:
Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun held talks with US Secretary of State Marco Rubio in Washington on Sunday, as Beirut seeks American backing to end fighting between Israel and Hezbollah and secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon. Aoun told Rubio that Lebanon and the US need to work together to implement the US-backed agreement signed by Lebanon and Israel on June 26, starting with an initial Israeli troop withdrawal. Rubio praised Lebanon's 'determined efforts to reclaim Lebanon's sovereignty, disarm Hezbollah and dismantle its terrorist infrastructure'.

Aoun is expected to meet President Trump on Tuesday and plans to present a written proposal outlining how Hezbollah's arsenal could be dismantled while securing Israel's withdrawal. The visit — the first by a Lebanese head of state to Washington since 2009 — comes at a pivotal moment: Israeli troops remain in parts of southern Lebanon months after the latest war, hundreds of thousands remain displaced, and Hezbollah has rejected Lebanon's direct negotiations with Israel.

The challenge for Aoun is considerable. Israel insists it will not fully withdraw unless Hezbollah is disarmed and demilitarised. Hezbollah rejects both disarmament and Lebanon's direct talks with Israel. Aoun, a former army commander elected after more than two years without a head of state, has staked his presidency on restoring the state's 'monopoly on arms' — but delivering on that promise requires both Israeli cooperation and Hezbollah compliance, neither of which is forthcoming.
sections.whyItMatters: Aoun's Washington visit is the first by a Lebanese president in 17 years and represents a calculated bet that Trump can pressure Israel into withdrawing. But the fundamental deadlock remains: Israel wants Hezbollah disarmed before withdrawal; Hezbollah won't disarm while Israeli troops occupy Lebanese territory.

sections.heading: Guyana Ferry Disaster, India's Education Protests, and Norway Wildfire
sections.body:
A ferry carrying 116 passengers and 17 crew sank off the coast of Guyana overnight, with 67 people rescued so far including 15 children. The MV Barima, built in 1939, capsized near Iron Punt while travelling from Georgetown to Port Kaituma. The captain is among the rescued and has returned to assist the search, which has more than doubled its area. Dozens remain missing.

In India, thousands of students, parents, and activists are set to march on parliament on Monday following a call from the Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) for education reforms. The satirical movement, founded by Abhijeet Dipke in May, has grown into a mass protest over exam paper leaks and irregularities that saw a crucial medical entrance test cancelled, affecting 2.28 million candidates. A retest was held on 21 June but more than 20 students reportedly died by suicide. Activist Sonam Wangchuk, on day 21 of a hunger strike, was forcibly taken to hospital on Saturday; CJP founder Dipke has now begun his own indefinite fast. The protesters are demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan, and after Wangchuk's forced removal, have escalated to also demanding PM Modi's resignation.

In Norway, more than 100 homes have been destroyed by a massive wildfire in Drammen, about 30 miles west of Oslo. Hundreds have been evacuated, with firefighters working to contain a blaze that has spread to a nearby forest. Police said the fire was 'still not under control'. A Red Cross worker attributed the rapid spread to recent dry heat conditions.
sections.whyItMatters: Three distinct crises on three continents: Guyana's ferry disaster raises questions about maritime safety on a vessel built in 1939; India's CJP movement is the most significant student-led protest since the farmers' agitation and directly challenges Modi's government on accountability; Norway's wildfire is another signal of Europe's escalating extreme weather pattern.

bottomLine: The Iran-US war entered its eighth day with a third American soldier killed and strikes now directly involving Jordan and Kuwait, while both sides show no sign of returning to negotiations — Iran's supreme leader declared US signatures 'worthless' and the US continues bombing Iranian infrastructure including a nuclear site. Russia escalated its aerial assault on Ukraine with one of the largest ballistic missile barrages of the war even as Ukraine struck back at Russian oil export infrastructure. In Israel, serving cabinet ministers openly marched for Gaza settlements, crossing a line from rhetoric to on-the-ground political mobilisation ahead of October elections. Lebanon's president made a historic visit to Washington to seek Trump's help implementing a deal that Israel and Hezbollah both resist. And in India, a grassroots education protest movement is converging on parliament with demands that have already escalated to calling for the prime minister's resignation.
